title: AI in law firms entering its closing summaries

raw_body:
In an interview with Artificial Lawyer, Paris-based AI-native consulting firm owner, Olivier Chaduteau, set out a three-part account of the current state of AI in the legal sector. At first, lawyers dismissed AI as irrelevant to expert work. In the second, organisations bought licences to LLMs to signal activity to partners and/or clients, but little […]
The post AI in law firms entering its closing summaries appeared first on AI News.
speedrun: AI is making inroads into the legal sector, as discussed by Olivier Chaduteau, a consultant in Paris. Initially, lawyers viewed AI as irrelevant, but now firms are acquiring licenses for large language models (LLMs) to demonstrate engagement with technology. This shift indicates a growing acceptance of AI's role in legal work, which could reshape how legal services are delivered and perceived.
why_it_matters: Law firms are starting to embrace AI, which could enhance efficiency and reduce costs for clients by automating routine tasks.
This trend reflects a broader change in the legal industry, signaling a move towards more tech-driven practices that could redefine client interactions and service delivery.

lenses.eli12: AI is slowly being accepted in law firms, shifting from skepticism to exploration. Think of it like a chef learning to use a new kitchen gadget; at first, they might resist, but eventually, they see how it can improve their cooking. This matters because it could lead to faster legal processes and lower costs for everyone involved.
lenses.pm: For product managers and founders, this trend highlights a growing user need for AI solutions in legal practices. By integrating LLMs, firms could streamline operations and enhance client communication. This opens opportunities for creating tools that support legal professionals in their work.
lenses.engineer: Chaduteau outlines the evolution of AI in law, noting that firms are now investing in LLMs to showcase technological engagement. This shift suggests a potential increase in efficiency for legal tasks, although the specific models and benchmarks being adopted remain unclear. Engineers could explore developing tailored solutions that meet the unique demands of the legal sector.
